*Projection based on 2023 prison population. Includes people in prison with sentences more than one year who have not already been released on parole and excludes people with life sentences.

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In West Virginia, indeterminate sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. There is no standard ratio between minimum and maximum terms. “Definite sentences” include terms to parole-release eligibility that are set by statutory formula at 25 percent.¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility Most recent data shows that 31 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility, while another 68 percent have yet to reach their parole eligibility. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 580 percent. Our forcasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will decrease by 3 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 89 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 40 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 60 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people in prison past parole eligibility were incarcerated for property (35 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 53 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who's most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: West Virginia *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, January 2022), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/west_virginia_doi_report_7_10_22.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/west_virginia_doi_report_7_10_22.pdf)

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43.

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.WV Division of Corrections & Rehabilitation, FY 2023 Annual Report (Charleston, WV: West Virginia Division of Corrections & Rehabilitation, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://dcr.wv.gov/Documents/FY23%20ANNUAL%20REPORT%20WVDCR.pdf, 33. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 12 percent, changing from 6,681 in 2010 to 5,847 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 84 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 90 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 34 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 49 percent of people in prison were in prison for violent offenses and 51 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people in prison were incarcerated for property (23 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 41 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, the number of people released from prison increased 13 percent, dropping from 2,970 in 2010 to 3,342 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In the most recent year of data available, 70 percent of parole-eligible people released in West Virginia were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 319 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. 66 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 86 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 84 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 40 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 24 percent of people released from prison were in prison for violent offenses and 76 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people released from prison were incarcerated for property (36 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Parole Release ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_black_west_virginia.png){width=1190} ```{=html} Black, non-Hispanic people were 20 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility compared to White people, when accounting for population sizes in West Virginia.

``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_west_virginia.png){width=1185} ```{=html} Hispanic people were 30 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility compared to White people, when accounting for population sizes in West Virginia.
Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.
```

National Corrections Reporting Program and CSG Justice Center estimates, 2019

``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_male_west_virginia.png){width=1182} ```{=html} Male were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.4 times higher than females, when accounting for prison population sizes in West Virginia.
```

Black people and Hispanic people spend less time behind bars after becoming eligible for parole than White people. Black people spent on average 7 less months past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 1.6 less years past parole eligibility compared to White people.

Black people and Hispanic people spend less time behind bars than White people. Black people spent on average 2 less months in prison, and Hispanic people spent on average 1.1 less years in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}

The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and race and ethnicity. The largest disparity was observed among aggravated or simple assault offenses, where Black people spent on average 8 more months in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: {.cell} :::
## Notes on Disparities **Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes** In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex. The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility. **Metrics Displayed in Visualizations** Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex: - **RRI of Parole Release:** This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole. - **Average Time Past Parole Eligibility:** For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups. - **Average Total Time Served:** We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities. **Data Considerations** Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology. Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states: - Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations. - Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals. - Including 'Other' Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis. **Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation** Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis. ::: --- title: "West Virginia" ---
